question.text: How do interest rate changes affect call and put option premiums differently?

answer.text: Higher interest rates increase call premiums (positive Rho) and decrease put premiums (negative Rho) — because higher rates increase the present value advantage of delaying purchase (call) and decrease the present value advantage of delaying sale (put)
Interest rate changes affect calls and puts equally
Higher rates decrease both call and put premiums
Interest rates only matter for IR options, not equity or currency options

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Higher rates: Call value increases (forward price of underlying increases; also, PV of strike decreases for the call buyer). Put value decreases (PV of strike received decreases). This effect is larger for long-dated options and smaller for short-dated. For currency options, BOTH domestic AND foreign rates matter. **The Trap:** Calls and puts are affected in OPPOSITE directions by rate changes. The effect applies to ALL options (equity, currency, commodity), not just IR options. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII, I, IV, and XVI all discuss Rho. For currency options (I Ch4), the Garman-Kohlhagen model explicitly accounts for both interest rates. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** During rate hike cycles, long-dated call premiums rise and put premiums fall — factor this into long-dated hedging strategies to avoid overpaying for protection.
